JAKE KNAPP: Absolutely. That’s the core of the "prototype mindset." (p. 168) We're not building a perfect, polished product on Thursday. We’re creating a façade – just enough to make it appear real to customers so we can get their honest reactions. (p. 169, 230) It could be Keynote slides, a physical mock-up, or even just a brochure. The goal is to build just enough to learn, but not more. Goldilocks quality, as we call it. (p. 170)

INTERVIEWER: "Goldilocks quality." I like that. But what if the problem is really complex, say, a hardware product or a sophisticated service? Can you really "fake" that convincingly in a day?

JAKE KNAPP: Almost certainly. We've seen it work for hardware, for complex software, for services. You can modify an existing product, use 3D printing, or create what we call a "Brochure Façade." (p. 185) The key is to focus on what you need to learn. For example, with Savioke’s hotel robot, we prototyped interactions using an iPad on a wheeled cart to test how guests would react, long before the robot was fully functional. (p. 178-179)

INTERVIEWER: And when it comes to testing these prototypes on Friday, you’re very specific: five customer interviews. (p. 197) Why five? Why not three, or ten for more data?

JAKE KNAPP: We’ve found, and research by folks like Jakob Nielsen backs this up, that five is the magic number. (p. 197-198) With five one-on-one interviews, you'll see the major patterns emerge. More than that and you get diminishing returns for the time invested in a single day. Fewer, and it's hard to spot those crucial patterns.

INTERVIEWER: So, the team watches these five interviews together. (p. 218) What happens if the prototype bombs? If all five users are confused or hate it? That must be demoralizing.

JAKE KNAPP: That’s where our philosophy of "a winner every time" comes in. (p. 223) If the prototype fails, it’s an "efficient failure." The team learns, in just five days, that they were on the wrong track, saving potentially months or years of wasted effort building the wrong thing. That knowledge is incredibly valuable and allows the team to confidently refocus. Slack, for example, had an efficient failure with their "Bot Team" idea, which saved them a huge undertaking. (p. 221, 223)

INTERVIEWER: "Efficient failure." It’s a good reframe. You also provide very detailed checklists, comparing running a sprint to baking a cake: "If you don’t follow the recipe, you might end up with a disgusting mess." (p. 231) Is the process really that rigid? Is there no room for adaptation?

JAKE KNAPP: For your first few sprints, we absolutely recommend following all the steps. The structure is there for a reason – each step builds on the last, and skipping parts can compromise the outcome. Just like you wouldn't skip eggs in a cake. (p. 231) Once you’ve got it down, sure, feel free to experiment, just like an experienced baker might. But the core process – map, sketch, decide, prototype, test – is proven.

INTERVIEWER: You mention experienced bakers. What about teams with less experience, or even a facilitator running their first sprint? Can they pull this off?

JAKE KNAPP: Yes, absolutely. We wrote the book to be a comprehensive guide. If you follow the process, you have everything you need. (FAQ: Can I facilitate a sprint without any experience?) We’ve seen it work in all sorts of contexts, from startups to big companies, nonprofits, even classrooms. (p. 229-230)

INTERVIEWER: You’re quite insistent on the five-day structure. The FAQ section mentions you don't recommend one, two, or even three-day sprints, and are hesitant about four-day ones. (FAQ: Can we run a one-, two-, or three-day sprint?) Why such a hard line?

JAKE KNAPP: Because compressing it usually means you either don't finish prototyping and testing, which is where the real learning happens, or you work crazy hours and burn out. (FAQ) The five-day structure is designed to be intense but sustainable, allowing for deep work and critical learning without exhausting the team. You're home for dinner each night. (FAQ: Do sprints require long hours?)

INTERVIEWER: One of your key ideas is to "start slow so you can go fast." (p. 230) This seems to be embodied by dedicating all of Monday to mapping the problem and agreeing on a target before even thinking about solutions. Can you elaborate on why that initial "slowness" is so critical?

JAKE KNAPP: If you jump straight into solutions without a shared understanding of the problem and a clear target, teams often end up talking past each other or solving the wrong thing. Monday is about building that shared understanding. By mapping the problem, listing sprint questions, and picking a specific target, the team aligns. (p. 55-87) This careful setup allows us to move with incredible speed and focus for the rest of the week.

INTERVIEWER: So, the sprint isn't just about speed; it's about focused speed built on a solid foundation. You also talk about getting the "Decider" to delegate if they can't be there for the whole sprint. (p. 31) How crucial is consistent leadership presence?

JAKE KNAPP: It's very important. Decisions need to stick. If the primary Decider can't be there, appointing a delegate who can participate fully and make those binding decisions is key. (p. 31) This ensures momentum isn't lost and the team doesn't get stalled waiting for approvals.

INTERVIEWER: Jake, this has been incredibly insightful. It's clear the sprint process, while structured, is designed to unlock a team's potential in a very focused way. One last thought: you mention the Wright brothers. (p. 227-228) They didn't use sprints, but you say they used a similar toolkit. What’s the core parallel you see?

JAKE KNAPP: The Wright brothers had an ambitious goal. They broke it down into big questions, prototyped solutions for specific challenges, tested them, learned from failures (they crashed a lot!), and kept iterating. (p. 228) That cycle of forming a question, building a prototype, and running a test became their way of life. Sprints aim to instill that same powerful habit in teams.

INTERVIEWER: A powerful habit indeed. Jake Knapp, thank you for sharing the thinking behind the Sprint.

JAKE KNAPP: My pleasure. Thanks for having me.

INTERVIEWER: Jake, your book outlines a "Five-Act Interview" structure. It sounds very methodical. What was the driving force behind formalizing customer interviews to such a degree, especially for fast-moving startups?

JAKE KNAPP: We found that startups needed a reliable, repeatable way to get deep insights quickly. Michael Margolis, our research partner at GV, developed this structure over years of experience. The Five-Act Interview ensures that even teams new to research can cover all the essential bases, from making the customer comfortable to getting actionable feedback on a prototype, all within a short timeframe. It's about making a practiced art learnable.

INTERVIEWER: You claim that *anyone* can conduct these interviews, even a CEO. That's a bold statement. Surely, there's a certain finesse required that not everyone possesses?

JAKE KNAPP: It's true that Michael is an excellent conversationalist with natural gifts, but the structure is designed to guide anyone. We've seen product managers, engineers, designers, salespeople, and yes, even CEOs, successfully conduct these interviews. The script, prepared on Thursday, makes the interviews easier to run and consistent, which is key to spotting patterns. It’s less about innate talent and more about following the process and adopting the right mindset.

INTERVIEWER: Speaking of the process, one rather intriguing piece of advice is for the interviewer to say, "I didn't design this," even if they actually did. Isn't that a bit disingenuous?

JAKE KNAPP: (Chuckles) It might seem that way, but the goal is paramount: to get frank, candid feedback. Customers are often hesitant to criticize something if they think they'll hurt the feelings of the person who poured their heart into it. By creating that slight separation, even if it's a white lie, you make it easier for them to be honest. The prototype is being tested, not the interviewer's ego.

INTERVIEWER: So, the truth is secondary to unvarnished feedback in this context?

JAKE KNAPP: It's about creating an environment where the customer feels completely free to express their genuine thoughts and reactions. If the interviewer is perceived as emotionally invested, it can inadvertently stifle that honesty. The focus is on learning what works and what doesn't for the *customer*.

INTERVIEWER: Another technique Michael Margolis uses is what you call "broken questions," where the interviewer trails off. Can you give an example and explain why that's more effective than a direct question?

JAKE KNAPP: Absolutely. Instead of asking, say, "Were you confused by the pricing information there?", which is leading, Michael might say, "So, what..." and then trail off. The customer then fills in the blank, perhaps with, "Well, I was just surprised to see that the prices were so high." It encourages them to think aloud without any prompting from the interviewer about *what* they should be thinking about. It avoids biasing their response and often elicits more genuine, top-of-mind reactions.

INTERVIEWER: It sounds like a subtle form of conversational jujitsu. Does it require a lot of practice to master not finishing your own sentences?

JAKE KNAPP: Like any skill, it gets easier with practice. The key is to be comfortable with silence. Often, interviewers feel the need to fill every pause, but allowing those moments can give the customer space to articulate something really important. The broken question is a tool to gently nudge them to share what they're already reacting to.

INTERVIEWER: You also emphasize a "curiosity mindset." Many would say curiosity is just a personality trait. How do you teach someone to be genuinely curious?

JAKE KNAPP: We believe it's an outlook that can be embodied and even learned. It's not just about passively wondering. It's about actively focusing on surprising details, always asking "why?" instead of jumping to conclusions. We even suggest practical things like using body language – smiling, leaning in – to make yourself friendlier and more receptive, which in turn can foster that curious state. Before each interview, anticipating the interesting information you'll learn also helps.

INTERVIEWER: The Airbnb story is quite dramatic – from $200 a week to explosive growth, all sparked by customer interviews. Is that a common level of impact, or is it an outlier meant to inspire?

JAKE KNAPP: The Airbnb story is certainly a powerful testament, and Joe Gebbia himself described those initial interviews as "agonizing and enlightening." While we can't promise every startup will become Airbnb, we can promise that the process will be enlightening. Those interviews revealed flaws the founders couldn't see, even on simple things like picking a date on a calendar. It showed them how the product looked through their customers' eyes. That kind of insight is invaluable for any product.

INTERVIEWER: So, the interviews weren't about abandoning their vision, but refining it?

JAKE KNAPP: Exactly. Joe said, "There’s this gap between the vision and the customer. To make the two fit, you have to talk to people." Listening to customers gave them the knowledge they needed to combine with their vision, to close that gap and make a product that worked for real people. It’s about making the vision viable.

INTERVIEWER: You mention the interviewer shouldn't take notes, as the rest of the team in the sprint room handles that. What's the rationale there? Does it free up the interviewer to be more present?

JAKE KNAPP: Precisely. The interviewer's primary role is to be a good host, to build rapport, ask open-ended questions, and keep the customer thinking aloud. Trying to simultaneously conduct the interview and take comprehensive notes can be distracting and detract from the quality of the interaction. With the team observing and note-taking, the interviewer can fully focus on the customer and the flow of conversation.

INTERVIEWER: One of your debrief questions is, "If you had three magic wishes to improve this product, what would they be?" Why "magic wishes"?

JAKE KNAPP: The "magic wishes" question is a great way to help customers articulate their reactions and desires without being constrained by perceived feasibility. It’s not about turning product planning over to them, but it encourages them to think broadly and highlight what they found lacking or what would truly delight them. It often uncovers unmet needs or significant improvement areas.

INTERVIEWER: You strongly advise against multiple-choice or yes/no questions, calling them "leading questions in disguise." Could you elaborate on why they're so problematic?

JAKE KNAPP: Multiple-choice questions inherently limit the range of possible answers. If you ask, "Would you be ready to sign up now, or do you need more information?", you're assuming the customer wants one of those two options. They might be thinking something entirely different, like "This isn't right for my company." An open-ended question like, "Now that you’ve seen the site, what are you thinking?" gives them the space to provide a much richer, more honest answer. The "Five Ws and One H" questions – who, what, where, when, why, how – are far more effective for eliciting genuine thoughts.

INTERVIEWER: The text mentions a "Goldilocks quality" prototype. Could you briefly explain what that entails for those unfamiliar?

JAKE KNAPP: Ah, yes. A "Goldilocks quality" prototype is one that's realistic enough for customers to react to naturally, but not so polished that you've wasted a lot of time on something that might be wrong. It should look and feel real enough that the customer forgets it isn't the finished product once they start using it. This quality is crucial because it helps elicit genuine user behavior and feedback.

INTERVIEWER: You also talk about the physical setup for interviews being quite simple – a laptop, a webcam. Was this a conscious decision to make research more accessible and less intimidating for startups?

JAKE KNAPP: Absolutely. The goal is to lower the barrier to getting customer feedback. You don't need a special lab or expensive tech. A regular laptop, a webcam, and simple video meeting software are often all you need, even for mobile devices or hardware. This simplicity means teams can focus on the conversation and insights, not on complex logistics. It makes the whole process faster and more adaptable.

INTERVIEWER: Finally, Jake, based on your extensive experience with these sprints and interviews, what's the single most common mistake or overlooked aspect you see teams make when they first try to implement this kind of customer interviewing?

JAKE KNAPP: That's a great question. I think it often comes down to not fully embracing the "curiosity mindset" and, related to that, talking too much or trying to "sell" the prototype. The interviewer’s job is to listen and understand, not to defend the design or guide the customer to a specific conclusion. It's about being genuinely open to learning from the customer's perspective, even if it's uncomfortable or challenges your assumptions. Letting the customer talk, even through silences, is far more valuable than filling the air.

INTERVIEWER: It sounds like the interviewer needs a good dose of humility.

JAKE KNAPP: Humility and intense curiosity, absolutely. You're there to learn what you don't know, and the customer is your best teacher in that moment.

INTERVIEWER: Jake, thanks for joining us. Your book, "Sprint," details a fascinating process, but I want to jump straight to Thursday – prototype day. You make a pretty bold claim: 90% of the time, teams should use Keynote. For everything from apps to medical reports. That sounds almost too simple.

JAKE KNAPP: It does sound a bit crazy, doesn't it? But after over a hundred sprints, Keynote has consistently proven itself. The core idea is that your prototype doesn't need to be a real, functional product; it just needs to *appear* real for the purpose of testing on Friday. Keynote is fantastic for this because it's fast. It has easy layout tools, handles text, images, even clickable hotspots and animations. You can create something that looks and feels surprisingly polished in a very short amount of time.

INTERVIEWER: You explicitly advise against using a team's regular, often highly specialized, tools. You say they're "too perfect—and too slow." Can you elaborate on why perfection is a hindrance in this context?

JAKE KNAPP: Absolutely. When you're using your everyday professional tools, whether it's sophisticated design software or complex coding environments, there's an inherent pressure and tendency to get bogged down in details that don't matter for the prototype. You start worrying about pixel perfection, code efficiency, supply chains, brand guidelines – things that are vital for a final product, but will completely derail you when you only have one day to build a testable facade. The goal is speed and learning, not a production-ready asset.

INTERVIEWER: You mentioned Foundation Medicine, a cancer diagnostics company. They prototyped a paper medical report using Keynote. How does that work? I imagine medical professionals might expect something more… technical.

JAKE KNAPP: That’s a great example of Keynote's versatility. For Foundation Medicine, the 'product' was the information presented in the report. We used Keynote to design the layout, typography, and structure of that report. We then printed it out. The oncologists we showed it to were reacting to the clarity, usefulness, and presentation of the medical data, not the software it was created in. It just needed to look like a plausible, professional report, which Keynote handles beautifully.

INTERVIEWER: Another intriguing concept is the "Brochure Façade." You suggest that for physical products, sometimes it's better to prototype the marketing materials rather than the product itself. Why is that?

JAKE KNAPP: This is one of our favorite shortcuts! Often, a customer's first interaction with a new physical product isn't with the product itself, but with how it's presented – a website, a video, a brochure. Prototyping these marketing materials allows you to quickly test the core value proposition, which features resonate, whether the price point seems right, and how customers react to the *promise* of the product. And guess what? Keynote is excellent for mocking up websites, brochures, or sales decks.

INTERVIEWER: So, for screen-based things like apps or websites, Keynote or PowerPoint. For paper-based items like reports or brochures, again Keynote or PowerPoint. This reliance on presentation software seems almost like a universal solvent for prototyping challenges.

JAKE KNAPP: It often is, especially when speed and realism are paramount. These tools are designed for visual communication and can be quickly adapted. For websites, you might even use something like Squarespace. But the principle is the same: use what allows you to create a believable front-end rapidly.

INTERVIEWER: What about services? Like customer support or medical care. You can't exactly mock that up in Keynote.

JAKE KNAPP: You're right. For services, we recommend writing a script and using your sprint team as actors. The team members play the roles of service providers and customers, acting out the service interaction. This helps you test the flow, the communication, and the overall experience. It’s a very different kind of prototype, but the goal is the same: simulate the experience to get feedback.

INTERVIEWER: Let's talk about the team roles for Thursday. You emphasize having a dedicated "Writer." Why is this role so critical, especially if you have designers and engineers who can also write copy?

JAKE KNAPP: It's impossible to make a realistic prototype with unrealistic text. Placeholder text like "lorem ipsum" or hastily written copy immediately breaks the illusion. A dedicated Writer focuses solely on crafting believable and appropriate language. This is especially vital in specialized industries. For Foundation Medicine, we had a product manager with domain expertise as our Writer. They could produce medically realistic text that designers or engineers might struggle with. The words are a huge part of making the prototype feel real.

INTERVIEWER: The "Asset Collector" role sounds a bit like a glorified gofer, but you call it "one of the keys to rapid prototyping." Why is it so important?

JAKE KNAPP: (Chuckles) "Glorified gofer" is a bit harsh, but I see why you'd say that! It's not the most glamorous, but it’s incredibly crucial for speed. Makers need photos, icons, sample content. If they have to stop what they're doing to search for these assets every time, the process grinds to a halt. The Asset Collector is out there, scouring the web, image libraries, or existing company materials to find these pieces, freeing up the Makers to focus on creation.

INTERVIEWER: One of the most counterintuitive roles seems to be the Interviewer, who on Thursday writes the interview script for Friday but, and this is the kicker, *shouldn't* work on the prototype. Why this deliberate detachment?

JAKE KNAPP: This is really important for the integrity of Friday's customer interviews. If the Interviewer has spent Thursday building the prototype, they become emotionally invested in it. They might inadvertently lead the customer during the interview, or betray their own feelings – disappointment if a feature isn't liked, or glee if it is. By keeping them separate from the creation, they can approach Friday's interviews with more objectivity, focusing purely on observing the customer's genuine reactions.

INTERVIEWER: You have Makers creating individual components, and then a "Stitcher" combines them. Is this just a technical role of putting pieces together, or is there more to it?

JAKE KNAPP: It’s much more than just copy-pasting. The Stitcher is responsible for making the prototype feel seamless and consistent from beginning to end. When different Makers create different parts, you can end up with variations in style, or inconsistencies in fake data like names or dates. The Stitcher ensures that, for instance, "Jane Smith" in one screen isn't "Jane Smoot" in another. They also look for typos and fix obvious errors. Their job is to elevate the realism, perhaps by adding small details, like we did for FitStar by adding a screenshot of an iPad's on-screen keyboard to simulate typing. They are the guardian of coherence and realism.

INTERVIEWER: You mention dedicating resources, a Maker and a Writer, to the "opening scene" – what happens *before* the customer interacts with the core prototype. Why is this pre-experience so important?

JAKE KNAPP: The opening scene sets the stage and provides context. It makes the whole scenario more believable. For Blue Bottle Coffee, our opening scene was a faked New York Times article about their service. This article helped frame how a real customer might encounter Blue Bottle. Without a realistic entry point, customers might be confused or the test might feel too artificial. It doesn't need to be overly elaborate, but it needs to be credible enough to ease the customer into the prototype experience naturally.

INTERVIEWER: And all this culminates in a trial run around 3 p.m. on Thursday. What's the main purpose of this, so late in the day?

JAKE KNAPP: The trial run is crucial. It’s a chance for the whole team, especially the Interviewer, to see the complete prototype and how it flows. The Stitcher typically walks everyone through it. We double-check against the storyboard to ensure everything is included and that it addresses our key sprint questions. It's also the last chance to catch any glaring errors, inconsistencies, or gaps while there's still a bit of time to fix them before Friday's customer interviews. It ensures everyone is on the same page and the prototype is ready for its real test.

INTERVIEWER: Going back to prototyping services, you said the sprint team acts it out. Does that really provide valid feedback? Aren't they biased?

JAKE KNAPP: That's a fair question. While there's an element of role-playing, the focus isn't on the acting quality, but on the service *script* and flow. Does the dialogue make sense? Are there awkward points in the interaction? Does the proposed service process solve the customer's problem effectively? The team members, by stepping into these roles, can quickly identify friction points or areas of confusion in the service design itself. It's about testing the structure of the service, not the performance. It's a way to make the intangible tangible enough to critique and refine.

INTERVIEWER: You describe Thursday as a "pretty darn satisfying" day where you start with a big task, follow a plan, and end with a finished prototype. That sounds quite different from typical workdays for many.

JAKE KNAPP: It often is! In our normal work routines, it's rare to have such a clear beginning, a focused plan of action, and a tangible, completed outcome all within a single day. The focused nature of Thursday, the collaboration, and the rapid creation of something that feels real can be incredibly rewarding. There’s a real sense of accomplishment, and it sets the stage perfectly for the learning that happens on Friday.

INTERVIEWER: Jake, this has been incredibly insightful. The emphasis on speed, pragmatic tool choices like Keynote, and the specific roles all paint a very clear picture of how to make prototype day effective. Thank you.

JAKE KNAPP: My pleasure. The goal is to get to learning as quickly as possible, and Thursday is all about building the most efficient vehicle for that learning.
